steps.title: The Final Days Are for Celebration
steps.body:
Most of this program has been about what needs to change — the repairs, the gaps, the growth edges.

But a thriving marriage needs something else: the ability to celebrate what's working. To savor good moments instead of rushing past them.

Research shows that couples who actively celebrate each other's wins — big and small — have significantly higher relationship satisfaction.

Not because they have fewer problems. Because they balance the hard with the good.

Today, you're not fixing anything. You're savoring. Recognizing. Celebrating.

A marriage that only works on problems
becomes a marriage that only sees problems.

Celebration isn't optional.
It's how you remind each other why you chose this.

questions.label:
Take a moment and list everything that's going right in your marriage right now.

Not in comparison to others. Not in a 'we should be grateful' way. Just factually — what's working?

• How do you support each other?
• What are you good at as a couple?
• What moments this week made you feel glad you're together?
• What's something your partner does that you take for granted?

Write it down. Let it land.

steps.title: The Science of Savoring
steps.body:
Savoring is the act of intentionally focusing on a positive experience to extend and deepen the joy it brings.

It sounds soft. But it's actually a powerful relationship practice.

When your partner does something you appreciate, savoring means:
• NOTICING it (instead of being on autopilot)
• NAMING it (instead of assuming they know)
• LETTING IT LAND (pausing to feel the appreciation)

Example:
You notice your partner made your coffee the way you like it.
Instead of: silence (autopilot)
Try: 'You remember how I like my coffee. That's such a small thing and it means a lot.'

Savoring turns a nice moment into a connection moment.

questions.label:
Write three specific things you appreciate about your partner RIGHT NOW:

1. Something they did recently (action)
2. Something about who they are (character)
3. Something you're grateful for in your marriage (overall)

Get specific. 'They're nice' is too vague. 'They brought me tea when I was stressed without me asking' is specific.

Tomorrow, you'll share these.

steps.question: Why is celebrating your partner's wins important for your marriage?
options: It keeps the relationship positive and prevents negativity
When you celebrate each other's wins, the other person feels supported and known
It distracts from the problems you should be working on
It makes your partner feel obligated to celebrate you back
feedback: That's partly true, but it's more specific than just 'keeping it positive.'
Yes. When you celebrate your partner's wins, you're saying 'I see you, I'm on your team, and your success matters to me.' That's a powerful form of connection.
Celebration isn't a distraction from problems — it's part of a balanced relationship that can handle both.
Celebration isn't a transaction. It's genuine — or it doesn't count.

steps.title: Capitalizing vs Ignoring
steps.body:
Your partner comes home excited: 'I got the project approved! My presentation went great!'

Ignoring response: 'Oh nice. Did you pick up the milk?'

Passive response: 'Cool.'

Active constructive response — stop what you're doing, make eye contact, and engage: 'That's amazing! Tell me everything — what did they say? How did you feel presenting it?'

Research shows how you respond to your partner's good news is one of the strongest predictors of relationship quality. Active, enthusiastic responses build connection. Ignoring or minimizing builds distance.

steps.title: Celebrate Today
steps.prompt:
Today, when your partner shares something positive — no matter how small — give them your full attention.

Stop what you're doing. Make eye contact. Engage. 'That's awesome — tell me more.'

And share the appreciation list you wrote. Say those three specific things out loud.

Write how you'll share your appreciation:
steps.body:
Today, when your partner shares something positive — no matter how small — give them your full attention.

Stop what you're doing. Make eye contact. Engage. 'That's awesome — tell me more.'

And share the appreciation list you wrote. Say those three specific things out loud.

Write how you'll share your appreciation:

steps.question: Couples who actively celebrate each other's good news are more resilient during hard times.
feedback: Correct. Research on 'capitalization' — how couples respond to good news — shows that active enthusiastic responses build 'emotional bank accounts' that sustain couples through conflict and stress.

Actually, yes — it's called capitalization in relationship science. When couples actively celebrate each other's wins, they build a reservoir of positive connection that supports them through difficult seasons.

steps.title: Tomorrow: The Final Day — Your Marriage Manifesto
steps.body:
27 days. You've diagnosed, repaired, connected, and designed. Tomorrow is the last day — the culmination of everything you've built.

You'll create your Marriage Manifesto: a declaration of who you are as a couple and what you're committed to building together.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
